item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That interview was just fascinating, you made the guy so interesting in just a few lines… I've seen DROVES of interviews that wouldn't be able to make you care about the interviewee even if they pointed a gun to your head. Bravo!

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Wikiwalk
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This is a mostly standalone anomaly, with not much similar content on site. However, it does mention the Ethics Committee, and a crosslink could be added there:

2041-11-██ SCP-2804-B enters the Charleston metropolitan area, affecting ~100 buildings per year for the next 35 years. Containment will likely entail some combination of mass amnesticization, preemptive destruction of buildings, and partial depopulation of Charleston via manipulation of the local economy1. SCP-2804 upgraded to Keter-class.

To:

2041-11-██ SCP-2804-B enters the Charleston metropolitan area, affecting ~100 buildings per year for the next 35 years. Containment will likely entail some combination of mass amnesticization, preemptive destruction of buildings, and partial depopulation of Charleston via manipulation of the local economy2. SCP-2804 upgraded to Keter-class.

If anyone has any other ideas, feel free to suggest them below.

Footnotes
1. Pending input from the Ethics Committee.
2. Pending input from the Ethics Committee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this well enough up until the interview, which is where I fell in love with it. It's creepy, but also very funny. The only issue I have is the same as positronicbrain's, I feel like the -A instances could be described more clearly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll take a look and see if I can't describe it a bit better. Glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, the origination date of this SCP is my birthday!

Anyways, the concept is pretty neat and isn't as boring as you'd expect a deck-building SCP to be. It ends off on a spooky note, which is nice. My only complaint is that the -A instances don't seem to be described very well. It's tough to get a picture of them in my head, and some information on what exactly caused them to be anomalous (in terms of the property size or whatever, not how the SCP itself functions).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The effect itself isn't all that interesting, apart from the projection of it hitting the North Pole. But that interview… The guy's mental block on decks is fascinating and slightly creepy, just for how off it is. If I can't get it out of my head in the near future, I may need to upvote.

Edit: Upvote was needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll do it tomorrow, if I remember. Cbf atm.

If bad SCPs are getting on your nerves to the point that you talk about them on unrelated discussion pages, I would wait until a piece has survived two days before checking it out from Most Recently Created. That way you'll only see pieces that are good enough to stick around, and you'll be able to assess them more level-headedly.

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Only large problem visible is the fact that it still comes up as access denied on the Browsing page, instead of having a name.

Chrom1312, some authors hold off on naming their new articles until the page reaches a certain rating. It's certainly not a "large problem".

1+ though, because this is much better than a lot of the shit I've seen.

Staff previously addressed the "better that shit I've seen" comment. It's essentially irrelevant, since not everyone has read the exact same collection of articles you have, and furthermore, "I've seen worse" doesn't really help the author or the community improve.

Please be more conscientious with your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty decent story. Only large problem visible is the fact that it still comes up as access denied on the Browsing page, instead of having a name. 1+ though, because this is much better than a lot of the shit I've seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, I get it now. That adds a whole other frightening layer to this I didn't even consider!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The margin of error is, indeed, pretty narrow. A degree north and you definitely hit Newfoundland, a degree South and you clip Scandinavia. But hey, that's what 'roughly' is for - I think there's enough wiggle room to avoid the US East Coast while doing that, but I'm not 100% sure. I'd need a very big map with Charleston marked to make that assessment.

If someone can show that no angle coming out of Charleston actually fits what I have written, I'll edit the article. But otherwise… eh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm having some trouble with the angle of 34 degrees north of east. That's pretty close to the general angle of the coastline in South Carolina, and I'm unaware of a line that traverses around 44 km of the Charleston metropolitan area, then enters the Atlantic Ocean, then stays in the ocean. At the very least, it'll likely clip Capes Fear, Lookout, and Hatteras in North Carolina.

I mean, I get the way you've plotted out the narrative: it starts out small, suddenly gets much worse, carves a path through a major city, then ceases to be a concern until far in the future where who knows? It'd clutter things up to talk about its path across Newfoundland and Iceland in the distant future, so yeah, no need there, but this thing is going to harass the Carolina coast for quite a long time.

My geometry/geography might be off and yeah, it's pedantic, but it's nagging me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maps are fucked up. Going in a cardinal direction is a lot different than going in a "line" around the Earth because it acts really weird at the poles. It can only be said to get to the North Pole at all because the Foundation decided that [insert distance] is close enough to count and rounded it off.

Let me put it another way — a straight line on a Mercator projection map gives you a consistent compass bearing, which is what -B has. The Mercator projection takes up more and more vertical space the closer you get to the poles, and cannot actually show the pole.

You can't proceed North, East, West, or any combination of the three from the North Pole — the only option is South, and then you have an arbitrary number of Souths to choose from. Maybe -B will stop near the North Pole and die because it can't keep going 34 degrees North of East in circles so tight. Maybe it'll just reverse course the way it came after a while. Maybe it'll pick a new South-esque direction and go that way. Maybe staying put long enough would build up enough energy to make the world asplode.

What it really boils down to is that the Foundation only knows THAT it moves and THAT it maintains a compass heading, not WHY it does those things or how important they are to it. So they just say they don't know what will happen.

Out-of-universe, we might surmise that since 2804 is basically an idea that got dislodged from Fly's mind, it travels that way because Fly thought of the direction between his house and the Wilkinson house in terms of a compass bearing. Some other things he probably thought included that decks should be sensibly constructed and stick within property lines. When 2804 couldn't adhere to those, it flipped out and made fucked up anomalies. Will it make anomalies when it can't go 34 degrees North of East any more? It seems quite likely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has a very creeping horror to it.

It's a lot of little subtle things that are so fundamentally wrong. I really really like this one.

Original, pulling, and darkly humorous. Well done.

Also, I like the idea of a classification of Indestructible. Seems like something the Foundation would do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2804-B's trajectory and activity past this point, if any, are unknown.

I suppose this is the only bit I have trouble with, since the Earth is spherical so it's likely to just loop around again, right? Or am I missing some nuance for why the Foundation can't hypothesize what happens?

Great article. A1 is weird as fuck, and I dig it.

EDIT: Also, this:

SCP-2804-A3 will return to its inactive state when any individual falls off of it.

is darkly humorous. Nice.

item.title: im author post?
item.content:encoded:
This one was inspired by the time that the guy who put up a deck at my family's house, who went by "Fly", came back ten years later and found that we'd taken it out and put in a patio, so he wrote about it on our sidewalk. Thanks to minmin for reviewing this in the forums.
